meta.title: Responding to 1H/1S
meta.description: Raise, bid 1NT, new suit, or jump raise

blocks.content:
## Partner Opens 1♥ or 1♠ -- Now What?

When partner opens 1♥ or 1♠, you already know something wonderful: they have at least 5 cards in that major (remember, SAYC uses 5-card majors). Your first question is always:

> **Do I have 3+ cards in partner's major?**

If yes, you've found a **fit** — at least 8 cards in the suit between you. That's exciting! If no, you'll need to describe your hand another way.

## With Support (3+ Cards in Partner's Major)

When you have a fit, your priority is to **raise partner's suit**. How high you raise depends on your strength:

### Single Raise: 6-10 Points
> Partner opens 1♠ → You bid **2♠**

This says: "I have 3+ spades and about 6-10 total points." It's a limited bid — partner now knows your approximate range.

**Example:** Partner opens 1♠. You hold ♠Q74 ♥J83 ♦KT52 ♣964
- 7 HCP, 3 spades. Bid **2♠**.

### Limit Raise: 10-12 Points (Invitational)
> Partner opens 1♠ → You bid **3♠**

This says: "I have 3+ spades and 10-12 points. I'm inviting you to bid game — go to 4♠ if you're near the top of your range."

**Example:** Partner opens 1♥. You hold ♠K8 ♥QJ74 ♦A953 ♣872
- 10 HCP, 4 hearts. Bid **3♥**.

### Game-Forcing Raise: 13+ Points
With 13+ points and support, you want to reach at least game (4♥ or 4♠). But you don't just jump to 4M right away — instead, you **bid a new suit first** to show your strength, and support partner's major later. This approach (called a "forcing bid") gives the partnership room to explore slam if opener also has extras.

**Example:** Partner opens 1♠. You hold ♠KJ5 ♥AQ83 ♦K74 ♣J62
- 14 HCP, 3 spades. Bid **2♥** first (a new suit, forcing), then support spades later.

### Direct Jump to Game: Weak with Great Support
Somewhat paradoxically, jumping directly to 4♥ or 4♠ often shows a **weak hand with excellent trump support** (5+ cards) — it's a preemptive raise designed to shut the opponents out.

**Example:** Partner opens 1♥. You hold ♠8 ♥KJ8743 ♦Q964 ♣53
- Only 7 HCP but 6-card heart support. Bid **4♥** as a preemptive raise.

## Without Support (0-2 Cards in Partner's Major)

When you don't have a fit, you need to explore other options:

### Bid a New Suit at the 1-Level: 6+ Points
If you have a 4+ card suit that you can bid at the 1-level (meaning it ranks higher than partner's suit), do so. This is forcing — partner must bid again.

**Example:** Partner opens 1♥. You hold ♠KQ85 ♥72 ♦A943 ♣J84
- 10 HCP, 4 spades. Bid **1♠**. (You can bid a new suit at the 1-level.)

### Bid a New Suit at the 2-Level: 10+ Points
Bidding a new suit at the 2-level requires more points because you're committing the partnership to a higher level. You generally need 10+ points and a good 4+ card suit.

**Example:** Partner opens 1♠. You hold ♠5 ♥K83 ♦AQ874 ♣J962
- 11 HCP, no spade support, a good 5-card diamond suit. Bid **2♦**.

### Bid 1NT: 6-10 Points, No Better Bid
When you have 6-10 points but can't raise partner's suit and can't bid a new suit at the 1-level, bid 1NT. In standard SAYC, this 1NT response is **not forcing** — partner may pass.

**Example:** Partner opens 1♠. You hold ♠83 ♥KJ7 ♦Q963 ♣T852
- 7 HCP, only 2 spades, no suit to bid at the 1-level. Bid **1NT**.

## Response Summary Table

| Your Hand | Points | Your Bid |
|-----------|--------|----------|
| 3+ card support | 6-10 | Raise to 2M |
| 3+ card support | 10-12 | Raise to 3M (invitational) |
| 3+ card support | 13+ | New suit first, then support |
| 5+ card support, weak | 6-10 | Jump to 4M (preemptive) |
| New suit available at 1-level | 6+ | Bid the new suit |
| New suit at 2-level | 10+ | Bid the new suit |
| Nothing else fits | 6-10 | 1NT |
| 0-5 points | Any | Pass |

problems.question: Partner opens 1♠. You hold ♠K83 ♥Q74 ♦J952 ♣T63. (7 HCP, 3 spades) What do you bid?
problems.explanation: You have 3-card spade support and 7 points -- a clear single raise to 2♠.

problems.question: Partner opens 1♥. You hold ♠AJ85 ♥6 ♦KQ73 ♣T84. (11 HCP, 1 heart) What do you bid?
problems.explanation: No heart support, but you have a 4-card spade suit you can bid at the 1-level. Bid 1♠ -- it's forcing and keeps the bidding low.

problems.question: Partner opens 1♠. You hold ♠Q9742 ♥84 ♦J73 ♣Q85. (6 HCP, 5 spades) What do you bid?
problems.explanation: 5-card support is great, but with only 6 HCP, a simple raise to 2♠ is right. You don't have the weakness-plus-length profile (typically needs even less HCP or more trumps) for a preemptive jump to 4♠.

problems.question: Partner opens 1♥. You hold ♠KJ3 ♥A85 ♦QT74 ♣K92. (13 HCP, 3 hearts) What do you bid?
problems.explanation: With 13 points and heart support, you want to reach game. But don't jump to 3♥ (that's only 10-12) or 4♥ (preemptive). Bid a new suit first (2♦ is forcing), then show heart support later.

problems.question: Partner opens 1♠. You hold ♠7 ♥Q84 ♦KJ5 ♣J97632. (8 HCP, 1 spade) What do you bid?
problems.explanation: Only 1 spade (no fit), 8 HCP, and your club suit isn't good enough for a 2-level response (which needs 10+ points). Bid 1NT.

reviewQuestions.question: Partner opens 1♠. You have 3 spades and 8 points. What do you bid?
reviewQuestions.explanation: With 3+ card support and 6-10 points, raise to 2♠ (a single raise).
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Well done! A simple raise with support and minimum values -- the bread and butter of responding.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: With 3-card support and 8 points, you raise to 2♠. That's the single raise, showing 6-10 points with a fit.

reviewQuestions.question: Partner opens 1♥. You have 11 HCP and 4 spades but only 1 heart. What do you bid?
reviewQuestions.explanation: With no heart support but a 4-card spade suit, bid 1♠ at the 1-level. It's forcing and shows your suit.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Exactly! Show your 4-card major at the 1-level -- it's cheap and forcing.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Without heart support, show your own suit. Bid 1♠ -- it's forcing and lets partner know about your spades.

reviewQuestions.question: Why would you bid a new suit with 13+ points and support for partner's major, instead of raising directly to game?
options: A direct raise to game would be too strong
A direct raise to 4M is preemptive (weak), so you bid a new suit first to show strength
You need to find a better trump suit
reviewQuestions.explanation: A direct jump to 4♥/4♠ is preemptive in SAYC. With a strong hand, bid a new suit first (forcing), then show support -- this leaves room to explore slam.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Great understanding! The jump to game is paradoxically weak, so strong hands take a detour through a new suit.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: In SAYC, jumping directly to 4♥/4♠ is preemptive (weak with lots of trumps). Strong hands bid a new suit first to keep options open.
